title: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System

items.subtopic: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System
items.title: Importance of Physical Examination Skills in Cardiovascular Assessment
items.content: Despite a decline in physical examination skills over recent decades, these remain crucial for assessing cardiovascular diseases. Classic cardiac findings are often unrecognized by many residents, and clinical performance does not necessarily improve with experience alone.
keyPoints: There is a notable decline in physical examination skills among students to faculty specialists.
Classic cardiac findings are recognized by only a minority of internal medicine and family practice residents.
Clinical performance may not predictably improve with experience due to the difficulty of acquiring new skills for busy practitioners.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System

items.subtopic: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System
items.title: Role of Physical Exam in Acute Coronary Syndromes and Heart Failure Prognosis
items.content: History and physical examination findings, such as heart rate, blood pressure, signs of pulmonary congestion, and mitral regurgitation (MR), are critical for bedside risk assessment in acute coronary syndromes. In heart failure, prognosis can be predicted using jugular venous pressure (JVP) and the presence or absence of a third heart sound ($ S_{3} $).
keyPoints: Heart rate, blood pressure, pulmonary congestion signs, and MR are important for risk assessment in acute coronary syndromes.
Prognosis of heart failure can be predicted using JVP and the presence or absence of an $ S_{3} $.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System

items.title: Recognizing Cyanosis and Its Implications
items.content: Cyanosis can indicate various cardiovascular conditions based on its type and location. Central cyanosis suggests significant right-to-left shunting, while peripheral cyanosis is often due to reduced extremity blood flow.
keyPoints: Central cyanosis occurs with significant right-to-left shunting at the heart or lungs level.
Peripheral cyanosis relates to reduced extremity blood flow, seen in severe heart failure, shock, or peripheral vascular disease.
Differential cyanosis affects lower but not upper extremities in patients with a large patent ductus arteriosus and secondary pulmonary hypertension.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.title: Identifying Skin Manifestations of Cardiovascular Disorders
items.content: Various skin conditions can suggest underlying cardiovascular or systemic disorders, such as hemochromatosis, lipid disorders, and connective tissue diseases.
keyPoints: Tan or bronze discoloration may indicate hemochromatosis associated with systolic heart failure.
Jaundice visible in the sclera suggests advanced right heart failure and congestive hepatomegaly.
Subcutaneous xanthomas are linked to hereditary lipid disorders, with palmar crease xanthomas specific for type III hyperlipoproteinemia.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.title: Assessing Head and Neck Features in Cardiovascular Contexts
items.content: Head and neck features can provide clues to cardiovascular conditions, including congenital heart disease and connective tissue disorders.
keyPoints: A high-arched palate is a feature of Marfan syndrome and other connective tissue diseases.
Bifid uvula may be seen in Loeys-Dietz syndrome.
Blue sclerae are characteristic of osteogenesis imperfecta.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Cardiovascular Clues from Physical Examination
items.content: Physical examination findings such as scars, venous patterns, and thoracic deformities can provide critical clues to underlying cardiovascular disorders.
keyPoints: Scars like midline sternotomy or thoracotomy may indicate prior cardiac surgeries.
Prominent venous collateral pattern suggests subclavian or vena caval obstruction.
Dusky head/neck with elevated venous pressure without pulsations indicates superior vena cava syndrome.
Pectus carinatum and pectus excavatum are associated with connective tissue diseases.
Barrel chest deformity suggests obstructive lung disease, especially with tachypnea and accessory muscle use.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.title: Abdominal Examination in Cardiovascular Assessment
items.content: Certain abdominal findings can indicate cardiovascular issues such as heart failure or valvular disease.
keyPoints: Epigastric cardiac impulse may be present in advanced obstructive lung disease.
Systolic pulsations over the liver suggest severe tricuspid regurgitation.
Splenic enlargement is a feature of infective endocarditis with prolonged symptoms.
Ascites can indicate chronic right heart failure, constrictive pericarditis, or hepatic cirrhosis.
Elevated JVP implies cardiovascular etiology; palpation sensitivity for abdominal aortic aneurysm decreases with body size.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.title: Extremity Examination for Cardiovascular and Systemic Clues
items.content: Examination of extremities can reveal signs of cardiovascular conditions, systemic diseases, or vascular issues.
keyPoints: Clubbing suggests central right-to-left shunting or endocarditis; hypertrophic osteoarthropathy indicates advanced lung/liver disease.
Holt-Oram syndrome presents with an unopposable thumb; Marfan syndrome may show arachnodactyly and positive wrist/thumb signs.
Janeway lesions are nontender hemorrhagic lesions in endocarditis; Osler's nodes are tender nodules on fingers/toes.
Lower extremity edema with elevated JVP indicates volume overload, seen in chronic heart failure or constrictive pericarditis.
Edema without jugular venous hypertension may result from hypoalbuminemia, lymphatic/venous obstruction, or venous insufficiency.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.title: Estimating Central Venous Pressure via Jugular Venous Pulsation
items.content: The jugular venous pressure (JVP) is a crucial bedside measurement for estimating volume status. The internal jugular vein is preferred over the external jugular due to its direct alignment with the superior vena cava and right atrium, although both can be used to differentiate between high and low central venous pressures.
keyPoints: JVP estimation involves measuring the vertical distance from the top of the jugular venous pulsation to the sternal inflection point (angle of Louis).
A JVP >4.5 cm at 30° elevation is considered abnormal.

The clavicle can be used as a reference for standardization, with venous pulsations above this level in the sitting position being clearly abnormal due to the distance between the clavicle and right atrium (at least 10 cm).
Bedside estimates of CVP are made in centimeters of water and must be converted to millimeters of mercury (1.36 cmH₂O = 1.0 mmHg) for correlation with hemodynamic norms.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics and Clinical Significance of the Jugular Venous Waveform
items.content: The jugular venous waveform has distinct components that can be used to assess cardiac function. The arterial pulse differs from the venous waveform, which is biphasic in sinus rhythm and changes with posture or inspiration unless elevated.
keyPoints: The $a$ wave reflects right atrial presystolic contraction and occurs after the P wave, preceding $S_1$. It's prominent in reduced right ventricular compliance and absent in atrial fibrillation.
A cannon $a$ wave indicates AV dissociation or right atrial contraction against a closed tricuspid valve, helping identify ventricular tachycardia.
The $x$ descent follows the $a$ wave, while the $c$ wave occurs during early ventricular systole. The $v$ wave represents atrial filling and is accentuated in tricuspid regurgitation (TR).
Kussmaul's sign, a rise or lack of fall in JVP with inspiration, is associated with conditions like constrictive pericarditis and restrictive cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Implications of Jugular Venous Waveform Abnormalities
items.content: Abnormal jugular venous waveforms can indicate specific cardiac conditions. The $v$ wave's height and the $y$ descent's characteristics provide insights into right atrial compliance and potential obstructions.
keyPoints: In TR, the $v$ wave is accentuated, and the $y$ descent is rapid; with severe TR, these waves merge, ventricularizing the waveform.
A prolonged or blunted $y$ descent suggests right ventricular inflow obstruction, as seen in tricuspid stenosis or pericardial tamponade.
Normally, venous pressure should fall by at least 3 mmHg with inspiration; deviations from this can indicate underlying cardiac issues.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.title: Abdominojugular Reflux Maneuver Predicts Pulmonary Artery Wedge Pressure
items.content: The abdominogujular reflux maneuver, performed by applying firm pressure over the right upper quadrant for >15 seconds, can predict a pulmonary artery wedge pressure >15 mmHg in heart failure patients. A positive response is indicated by a sustained rise of >3 cm in the jugular venous pressure (JVP).
keyPoints: Firm abdominal pressure applied over 10-15 seconds.
Positive response: sustained JVP rise >3 cm.
Predicts pulmonary artery wedge pressure >15 mmHg in heart failure.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.title: Elevated Jugular Venous Pressure and Heart Failure Prognosis
items.content: An elevated JVP indicates a right atrial pressure >10 mmHg, correlating with pulmonary artery wedge pressure >22 mmHg in advanced heart failure. Elevated JVP is associated with higher risks of hospitalization or death from heart failure.
keyPoints: Elevated JVP predicts right atrial pressure >10 mmHg.
Positive predictive value for PAWP >22 mmHg: 88%.
Prognostic significance in symptomatic and asymptomatic LV dysfunction.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.title: Blood Pressure Measurement Techniques and Considerations
items.content: Accurate blood pressure measurement requires proper technique, including arm positioning at heart level, using an appropriately sized cuff, and measuring after relaxation. Differences >10 mmHg between arms may indicate vascular disease.
keyPoints: Measure in seated position with arm at heart level.
Cuff size: bladder 80% length, 40% width of arm circumference.
Inflate to 30 mmHg above expected systolic pressure.
Systolic and diastolic pressures defined by Korotkoff sounds I and V.
Arm pressure difference >10 mmHg may indicate vascular disease.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.title: Understanding White Coat and Masked Hypertension
items.content: White coat hypertension involves elevated clinic blood pressure but normal out-of-clinic measurements, while masked hypertension shows the opposite pattern. These conditions can influence treatment decisions.
keyPoints: White coat hypertension: Clinic BP >130/80 mmHg on ≥3 occasions and non-clinic BP <130/80 mmHg on ≥2 occasions without target organ damage.
Masked hypertension: Normal or low clinic BP but elevated out-of-clinic BP, especially in patients with advanced atherosclerosis or evidence of target organ damage.
Higher systolic BP measured by 24-h ambulatory device is linked to increased cardiovascular disease risk and all-cause mortality.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 3)

items.title: Diagnosing Orthostatic Hypotension
items.content: Orthostatic hypotension, defined by a significant drop in blood pressure upon standing, can indicate autonomic insufficiency and is associated with conditions like diabetes mellitus or Parkinson's disease.
keyPoints: Orthostatic hypotension: Fall in systolic BP >20 mmHg or diastolic BP >10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing from supine position.
May lack compensatory tachycardia, suggesting autonomic insufficiency.
Commonly causes postural lightheadedness/syncope; exacerbated by age, dehydration, medications, and environmental factors.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 3)

items.title: Characteristics of Arterial Pulses in Cardiac Conditions
items.content: The arterial pulse's character can indicate specific cardiac conditions such as aortic stenosis or regurgitation, with distinct patterns like pulsus parvus et tardus and Corrigan's pulse.
keyPoints: Pulsus parvus et tardus: Weak and delayed pulse indicating severe aortic stenosis (AS).
Anacrotic pulse: Slow, notched, or interrupted upstroke in AS with possible thrill/shudder.
Corrigan's pulse: Sharp rise and rapid fall-off seen in chronic severe aortic regurgitation (AR).
Bifid or bisferiens pulse: Two systolic peaks, observed in advanced AR, HOCM, and IABP.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 3)

items.title: Understanding Pulsus Paradoxus
items.content: Pulsus paradoxus is a significant drop in systolic blood pressure during inspiration, greater than 10 mmHg. It's crucial for diagnosing conditions like pericardial tamponade and can be palpable when the pressure difference exceeds 15 mmHg.
keyPoints: Fall in systolic pressure >10 mmHg with inspiration
Conditions: Pericardial tamponade, massive pulmonary embolism, hemorrhagic shock, severe obstructive lung disease, tension pneumothorax
Palpable when pressure difference exceeds 15 mmHg
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 4)

items.title: Identifying Pulsus Alternans
items.content: Pulsus alternans is characterized by beat-to-beat variability in pulse amplitude, indicative of severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction. It's associated with an increased risk for arrhythmic events when coupled with T-wave alternans.
keyPoints: Beat-to-beat variability of pulse amplitude
Indicates severe LV systolic dysfunction
Associated with increased arrhythmia risk if T-wave alternans is present
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 4)

items.title: Detecting Ascending Aortic Aneurysms and Peripheral Arterial Disease
items.content: A pulsatile mass in the right parasternal area may indicate an ascending aortic aneurysm, necessitating imaging. Claudication symptoms and abnormal pulse oximetry can signal peripheral arterial disease.
keyPoints: Pulsatile mass in right parasternal area suggests ascending aortic aneurysm
Noninvasive imaging recommended for characterization
Claudication and >2% difference in oxygen saturation between fingers and toes indicate peripheral arterial disease
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 4)

items.title: Identifying Left Ventricular Enlargement
items.content: Left ventricular enlargement can be detected through physical examination by observing a leftward and downward displacement of the apex beat. A sustained apex beat suggests pressure overload, often seen in conditions like aortic stenosis (AS) or chronic hypertension.
keyPoints: Enlargement of the LV cavity is indicated by a leftward and downward displacement of an enlarged apex beat.
A sustained apex beat signifies pressure overload, such as in AS or chronic hypertension.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 5)

items.title: Recognizing Right Ventricular Pressure or Volume Overload
items.content: Right ventricular pressure or volume overload can manifest as a sternal lift. Confirmatory signs include cv waves in the jugular venous pulse for tricuspid regurgitation and a loud single or palpable P2 for pulmonary arterial hypertension.
keyPoints: Right ventricular pressure or volume overload may create a sternal lift.
Confirmatory signs: cv waves in the jugular venous pulse (TR) and/or a loud single or palpable P2 (pulmonary arterial hypertension).
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 5)

items.title: Understanding Splitting of S2
items.content: The splitting of the second heart sound (S2) provides insights into cardiac physiology and pathology. Physiologic splitting occurs with inspiration, increasing the A2-P2 interval due to delayed pulmonic valve closure. Pathologic conditions like pulmonary arterial hypertension narrow or eliminate this split, while fixed splitting is seen in atrial septal defects.
keyPoints: Physiologic S2 splitting increases during inspiration and narrows during expiration.
Narrowly split or singular S2 indicates pulmonary arterial hypertension.
Fixed splitting of S2 occurs with secundum ASD.
Reversed or paradoxical splitting is seen in conditions like left bundle branch block, severe AS, HOCM, and acute myocardial ischemia.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics of S1 Intensity
items.content: The intensity of the first heart sound (S1) is influenced by factors such as mitral valve mobility, left ventricular contractility, and PR interval. It is louder in conditions like rheumatic mitral stenosis or hyperkinetic states but softer with long PR intervals or LV dysfunction.
keyPoints: Loud S1 occurs with short PR intervals, high LV contractility, and early rheumatic MS.
Soft S1 results from long PR intervals, beta-blocker use, and advanced mitral stenosis.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 1)

items.title: Distinguishing Diastolic Sounds
items.content: Diastolic sounds like the opening snap in mitral stenosis and pericardial knock in constrictive pericarditis are key diagnostic clues. The timing of these sounds relative to S2 helps differentiate between conditions.
keyPoints: Opening snap (OS) follows shortly after S2, inversely related to the left atrial-left ventricular pressure gradient.
Pericardial knock occurs slightly later than OS and corresponds with abrupt cessation of ventricular expansion.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 1)

items.title: S3 Heart Sound: Indicative of Heart Failure in Older Patients
items.content: The third heart sound (S3) occurs during the rapid filling phase of ventricular diastole. While it can be normal in younger individuals, its presence in older patients is a red flag for heart failure.
keyPoints: Normal in children, adolescents, and young adults; signifies heart failure in older patients
Left-sided S3: low-pitched sound best heard over the LV apex
Right-sided S3: better heard over lower left sternal border, louder with inspiration
Predictive of cardiovascular morbidity and mortality in chronic heart failure
Equally prevalent among heart failure patients with preserved and reduced LV ejection fraction
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 2)

items.title: S4 Heart Sound: Indicator of Left Ventricular Hypertrophy or Ischemia
items.content: The fourth heart sound (S4) occurs during the atrial filling phase and indicates left ventricular presystolic expansion. It is common in conditions where atrial contribution to ventricular filling is significant.
keyPoints: Occurs during atrial filling phase of ventricular diastole
Indicates LV presystolic expansion
Common in chronic LV hypertrophy or active myocardial ischemia
Not present with atrial fibrillation
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 2)

items.title: Midsystolic Murmurs: Differentiating AS from Other Causes
items.content: Midsystolic murmurs begin after S1 and end before S2, often crescendo-decrescendo. Aortic stenosis is a common cause but can be difficult to assess in certain patients.
keyPoints: Begins after S1 and ends before S2; typically crescendo-decrescendo
Aortic stenosis (AS) is the most common cause in adults
Examination findings for severe AS: parvus et tardus carotid upstrokes, late-peaking grade 3 or greater midsystolic murmur, soft A2, sustained LV apical impulse, S4
Aortic sclerosis vs. stenosis: Doppler jet velocity ≤ 2.5 m/s in sclerosis
Other causes of midsystolic murmurs include pulmonic valve stenosis, HOCM, large ASD with left-to-right shunting, and states like fever, thyrotoxicosis, pregnancy, anemia
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 2)

items.title: Differentiating HOCM Murmur with Maneuvers
items.content: The murmur of hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM) can be distinguished from other causes based on its response to specific maneuvers. These include Valsalva, passive leg raising, and standing/squatting.
keyPoints: Maneuvers that decrease LV preload or increase LV contractility intensify the murmur of HOCM.
The murmur becomes louder during the strain phase of the Valsalva maneuver and after standing quickly from a squatting position.
The murmur softens with passive leg raising and when squatting.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 3)

items.title: Identifying Mitral Valve Prolapse (MVP) Murmur
items.content: A late systolic murmur at the apex, potentially preceded by nonejection clicks, indicates mitral valve prolapse (MVP). The click-murmur complex responds similarly to HOCM during Valsalva and stand/squat maneuvers.
keyPoints: The murmur of MVP is best heard at the apex with or without preceding nonejection clicks.
Differential radiation can help identify the specific leaflet involved by the myxomatous process.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 3)

items.title: Characteristics of Diastolic Murmurs
items.content: Diastolic murmurs always indicate structural heart disease. Acute severe aortic regurgitation (AR) presents with a soft, short murmur due to rapid rise in LV diastolic pressure.
keyPoints: Acute severe AR has a relatively soft and short-duration murmur.
Chronic severe AR is heard as a decrescendo, blowing diastolic murmur along the left sternal border.
Detection of subtle AR murmurs can be enhanced by auscultation during end-expiration with the patient leaning forward.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 3)

items.title: Distinguishing MS from Austin Flint Murmur
items.content: Mitral stenosis (MS) presents with a mid- to late-diastolic murmur, often accompanied by an opening snap (OS), whereas the Austin Flint murmur of chronic severe aortic regurgitation is similar but decreases in intensity after vasodilators. Vasodilator response can help differentiate these murmurs.
keyPoints: MS murmur: mid- to late-diastolic, low-pitched or rumbling, best heard over the apex in left lateral decubitus position
Austin Flint murmur: low-pitched mid- to late apical diastolic murmur
Vasodilators decrease Austin Flint murmur intensity; may increase MS murmur intensity due to increased cardiac output
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 4)

items.title: Characteristics of Continuous Murmurs
items.content: Continuous murmurs persist across systole and diastole, beginning in systole and enveloping the second heart sound. They can be challenging to distinguish from mixed valvular heart disease murmurs. Classic causes include patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), ruptured sinus of Valsalva aneurysm, and arteriovenous fistulas.
keyPoints: Continuous murmur: persists across systole and diastole
Classic example: PDA, heard in the second or third interspace near the sternal border
Other causes: ruptured sinus of Valsalva aneurysm, coronary/great vessel arteriovenous fistula, arteriovenous fistula for dialysis access
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 4)

items.subtopic: Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)
items.title: Dynamic Auscultation: Respiratory Influence on Murmurs
items.content: Right-sided murmurs increase with inspiration, while left-sided murmurs are louder during expiration. This distinction aids in differentiating the origin of heart murmurs.
keyPoints: Right-sided murmurs and sounds generally increase with inspiration (except PES).
Left-sided murmurs and sounds are usually louder during expiration.
Use respiratory changes to help localize murmur origins.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)

items.subtopic: Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)
items.title: Valsalva Maneuver: Murmur Characteristics
items.content: Most murmurs decrease in intensity during the strain phase of the Valsalva maneuver, except for HOCM and MVP. Right-sided murmurs return to baseline earlier than left-sided murmurs after release.
keyPoints: Most murmurs decrease in length and intensity during Valsalva.
HOCM becomes louder; MVP becomes longer and often louder.
Right-sided murmurs return to control intensity earlier than left-sided.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)

items.subtopic: Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)
items.title: Exercise: Murmur Intensity and Characteristics
items.content: Murmurs due to blood flow across normal or obstructed valves become louder with exercise. HOCM murmur often decreases with maximum hand grip, while left-sided S4 and S3 sounds are accentuated.
keyPoints: Murmurs of PS, MS, MR, VSD, AR increase with isotonic and submaximal isometric exercise.
HOCM murmur often decreases with nearly maximum hand grip exercise.
Left-sided S4 and S3 sounds are accentuated by exercise, especially in ischemic heart disease.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)

items.title: Prosthetic Heart Valve Dysfunction: Recognizing Changes
items.content: Changes in heart sounds or new murmurs can indicate prosthetic valve dysfunction, which may lead to rapid clinical deterioration. Specific murmur characteristics help differentiate between types of prosthetic valves and potential complications.
keyPoints: Bioprosthetic mitral valve dysfunction may present with a grade 1-2 midsystolic murmur at the left sternal border and a soft mid-diastolic murmur after exercise in the left lateral decubitus position.
A high-pitched or holosystolic apical murmur suggests pathologic MR due to paravalvular leak or leaflet degeneration, indicating the need for diagnostic noninvasive imaging.

Mechanical valve dysfunction may manifest as a decrease in the intensity of opening or closing sounds; a high-pitched apical systolic murmur with mechanical mitral prosthesis and diastolic decrescendo murmur with mechanical aortic prosthesis indicate paravalvular regurgitation.
Prosthetic valve thrombosis can present with signs of shock, muffled heart sounds, and soft murmurs in both systole and diastole.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Exercise (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosing Pericardial Disease: Key Physical Findings
items.content: A pericardial friction rub is highly specific for acute pericarditis but may be intermittent or difficult to elicit. Pulsus paradoxus exceeding 12 mmHg in the presence of a large effusion has high sensitivity and specificity for diagnosing pericardial tamponade.
keyPoints: A pericardial friction rub is nearly 100% specific for acute pericarditis but may be intermittent or difficult to elicit, with components typically heard during ventricular systole, rapid early diastolic filling, and late presystolic filling.

Pulsus paradoxus exceeding 12 mmHg in a patient with a large pericardial effusion can diagnose pericardial tamponade with 98% sensitivity, 83% specificity, and a positive likelihood ratio of 5.9 (95% CI 2.4–14).
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Exercise (part 2)

items.subtopic: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System
items.question: Which factor has contributed to a decline in physical examination skills among clinicians?
options: Increased use of noninvasive imaging studies.
More time devoted to mentored cardiovascular examinations.
items.explanation: The passage notes a decline in physical examination skills partly due to increased reliance on noninvasive imaging studies.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System

items.subtopic: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System
items.question: What is one method mentioned for improving competency in bedside examination skills?
items.explanation: Simulation-based training is mentioned as a technique to improve bedside examination skills.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System

items.subtopic: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System
items.question: What can help predict the prognosis of patients with heart failure?
options: Jugular venous pressure (JVP) and presence or absence of a third heart sound ($ S_{3} $).
items.explanation: The prognosis of patients with heart failure can be predicted based on JVP and the presence or absence of a third heart sound ($ S_{3} $).
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System

items.question: Which condition is associated with differential cyanosis affecting the lower but not the upper extremities?
options: Patent ductus arteriosus with secondary pulmonary hypertension
items.explanation: Differential cyanosis is associated with a large patent ductus arteriosus and secondary pulmonary hypertension causing right-to-left shunting at the great vessel level.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.question: What skin manifestation might suggest hemochromatosis in a patient with systolic heart failure?
items.explanation: An unusually tan or bronze discoloration of the skin may suggest hemochromatosis as a cause of associated systolic heart failure.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.question: Which ocular finding can be seen in patients with hyperthyroidism?
items.explanation: The ocular manifestations of hyperthyroidism have been well described.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.question: Which oral feature is characteristic of Tangier disease?
items.explanation: Orange tonsils are characteristic of Tangier disease.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.question: Which thoracic deformity should prompt careful auscultation for a murmur of aortic regurgitation?
options: Kyphosis with compensatory lumbar, pelvic, and knee flexion
items.explanation: The characteristically severe kyphosis and compensatory lumbar, pelvic, and knee flexion of ankylosing spondylitis should prompt careful auscultation for a murmur of aortic regurgitation.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.question: Which condition is suggested by the presence of systolic pulsations over the liver?
items.explanation: Systolic pulsations over the liver signify severe tricuspid regurgitation.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.question: What does clubbing of the fingers imply?
items.explanation: Clubbing implies the presence of central right-to-left shunting, although it has also been described in patients with endocarditis.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.question: What does an elevated JVP imply?
items.explanation: The finding of an elevated JVP implies a cardiovascular etiology.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.question: Which vein is preferred for estimating jugular venous pressure due to its direct alignment with the superior vena cava and right atrium?
items.explanation: The internal jugular vein is preferred because it is directly in line with the superior vena cava and right atrium.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.question: What reference point leads to systematic underestimation of central venous pressure?
items.explanation: The use of the sternal angle as a reference point leads to systematic underestimation of CVP.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.question: What is the conversion factor from centimeters of water to millimeters of mercury for estimating central venous pressure?
items.explanation: Bedside estimates of CVP are made in centimeters of water and must be converted to millimeters of mercury using the factor 1.36 cmH₂O = 1.0 mmHg.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.question: Which wave in the jugular venous waveform is absent with atrial fibrillation?
items.explanation: The a wave, which reflects right atrial presystolic contraction, is not present with atrial fibrillation.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.question: What maneuver can be used to predict a pulmonary artery wedge pressure >15 mmHg in patients with heart failure?
options: Firm abdominal pressure over the left upper quadrant
items.explanation: The abdominogujular reflux maneuver is used to predict a pulmonary artery wedge pressure >15 mmHg in patients with heart failure.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.question: What is the correct technique for measuring blood pressure?
options: Seated position, arm at heart level, feet on floor
Supine position, arm below heart level
items.explanation: Blood pressure is best measured in the seated position with the arm at the level of the heart and feet on the floor.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.question: What should be done if a significant blood pressure differential (>10 mmHg) is found between arms?
options: Consider potential subclavian artery disease or aortic dissection
Only measure in the arm with higher pressure
Use a larger cuff on both arms
items.explanation: A significant blood pressure differential may indicate subclavian artery disease, supraventricular aortic stenosis, aortic coarctation, or aortic dissection.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.question: What is the significance of an elevated jugular venous pressure (JVP) in patients with heart failure?
options: It indicates a lower risk of hospitalization
It predicts higher risk of subsequent hospitalization or death from heart failure
It suggests normal right ventricular filling
items.explanation: An elevated JVP is associated with a higher risk of subsequent hospitalization for heart failure, death from heart failure, or both.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following best defines 'white coat hypertension'?
options: Elevated clinic blood pressure and normal out-of-clinic blood pressure with evidence of target organ damage.
Normal or low clinic blood pressure but elevated out-of-clinic blood pressure, often in patients with advanced atherosclerotic disease.
At least three separate clinic-based measurements >130/80 mmHg and at least two non-clinic-based measurements <130/80 mmHg without evidence of target organ damage.
A fall in systolic pressure >20 mmHg or diastolic pressure >10 mmHg within 3 minutes upon standing.
items.explanation: White coat hypertension is defined by elevated clinic blood pressure and normal out-of-clinic blood pressure without evidence of target organ damage.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 3)

items.question: Which condition should be suspected when a patient has normal or low office blood pressure but elevated out-of-office blood pressure?
items.explanation: Masked hypertension is suspected when normal or low office blood pressure is accompanied by elevated out-of-office blood pressure.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 3)

items.question: What defines orthostatic hypotension?
options: A fall in systolic pressure >20 mmHg or diastolic pressure >10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing.
Elevated clinic blood pressure and normal out-of-clinic blood pressure.
Normal or low clinic blood pressure but elevated out-of-clinic blood pressure.
A weak and delayed pulse (pulsus parvus et tardus).
items.explanation: Orthostatic hypotension is defined by a fall in systolic pressure >20 mmHg or diastolic pressure >10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 3)

items.question: Which pulse characteristic is associated with severe aortic stenosis?
options: Sharp rise and rapid fall-off (Corrigan's or water-hammer pulse).
Weak and delayed pulse (pulsus parvus et tardus).
Normal carotid upstroke with no abnormalities.
items.explanation: Severe aortic stenosis is associated with a weak and delayed pulse (pulsus parvus et tardus).
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 3)

items.question: In which condition is pulsus paradoxus most commonly observed?
items.explanation: Pulsus paradoxus is most commonly associated with pericardial tamponade.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 4)

items.question: What condition is characterized by beat-to-beat variability of pulse amplitude?
items.explanation: Pulsus alternans is characterized by beat-to-beat variability of pulse amplitude.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 4)

items.question: Which imaging modality should be prompted for further evaluation if a prominent abdominal aortic pulse is appreciated?
items.explanation: Noninvasive imaging with ultrasound or computed tomography should be prompted for further evaluation of a prominent abdominal aortic pulse.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 4)

items.question: What is the significance of an arterial bruit extending into diastole?
items.explanation: An arterial bruit that extends into diastole usually indicates a severe obstruction.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 4)

items.question: Which condition is indicated by a palpable presystolic impulse corresponding to an $S_{4}$?
options: Reduced LV compliance and forceful atrial contraction
Rapid early filling wave in heart failure
Large LV aneurysm with ectopic impulse
Triple cadence beat at the apex
items.explanation: A palpable presystolic impulse corresponds to $S_{4}$ and indicates reduced left ventricular compliance and forceful atrial contraction.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 5)

items.question: What does a palpable third heart sound ($S_{3}$) indicate?
options: Pressure overload in the left ventricle
Reduced LV compliance and forceful atrial contraction
Rapid early filling wave, often seen in heart failure
Triple cadence beat at the apex
items.explanation: A palpable third sound ($S_{3}$) indicates a rapid early filling wave, commonly associated with heart failure.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 5)

items.question: What might cause a sternal lift during cardiovascular examination?
options: Right ventricular pressure or volume overload
items.explanation: A sternal lift can be caused by right ventricular pressure or volume overload.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 5)

items.question: Which condition is associated with a singular second heart sound ($S_2$)?
items.explanation: A singular $S_2$ is a feature of pulmonary arterial hypertension.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 1)

items.question: What causes the intensity of the first heart sound ($S_1$) to become softer in later stages of mitral stenosis?
options: Increased distance between stethoscope and cardiac event
items.explanation: $S_1$ becomes softer in later stages of mitral stenosis when the leaflets are rigid and calcified.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 1)

items.question: In which condition does the $A_{2}-P_{2}$ interval not change during the respiratory cycle?
items.explanation: Fixed splitting of $S_2$ occurs in patients with a secundum atrial septal defect (ASD).
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 1)

items.question: What is the timing relationship between an ejection sound and the carotid pulse?
options: After the onset of the carotid upstroke
Corresponds to the upstroke of the carotid pulse
Before the onset of the carotid upstroke
items.explanation: An ejection sound corresponds in timing to the upstroke of the carotid pulse.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 1)

items.question: In which patient population is a left-sided S3 considered a normal finding?
items.explanation: A left-sided S3 is normal in children, adolescents, and young adults.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 2)

items.question: Where is a right-sided S3 best heard?
options: Over the lower left sternal border
At the right upper sternal border
items.explanation: A right-sided S3 is usually better heard over the lower left sternal border.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 2)

items.question: What does an S4 indicate about ventricular filling?
items.explanation: An S4 occurs during the atrial filling phase of ventricular diastole and indicates LV presystolic expansion.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 2)

items.question: Which condition is associated with a decrescendo early systolic murmur?
items.explanation: Acute severe MR results in a decrescendo early systolic murmur.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 2)

items.question: Which maneuver will typically cause the systolic murmur of HOCM to intensify?
options: Standing quickly from a squatting position
items.explanation: The systolic murmur of HOCM becomes louder during the strain phase of the Valsalva maneuver.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 3)

items.question: Where is the murmur of aortic stenosis (AS) typically loudest?
options: Lower left sternal border to apex
items.explanation: The murmur of AS is typically loudest in the second right interspace with radiation into the carotids.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 3)

items.question: Which maneuver increases the intensity of a mitral valve prolapse (MVP) murmur?
options: Standing quickly from a squatting position
items.explanation: The click-murmur complex of MVP behaves similarly to HOCM during the Valsalva maneuver, becoming louder.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 3)

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of diastolic murmurs?
options: They always signify structural heart disease.
They indicate increased left atrial compliance.
They are typically associated with acute MR.
items.explanation: Diastolic murmurs always signify structural heart disease.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 3)

items.question: Which condition is characterized by a mid- to late-diastolic murmur that decreases in intensity after exposure to vasodilators?
options: Austin Flint murmur of chronic severe AR
items.explanation: The Austin Flint murmur decreases in intensity after vasodilators, unlike the murmur of mitral stenosis.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 4)

items.question: Which auscultatory finding is absent in patients with atrial fibrillation?
items.explanation: Presystolic accentuation is absent in patients with atrial fibrillation.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 4)

items.question: Which condition can be confused with mitral stenosis due to a similar low-pitched mid- to late apical diastolic murmur?
options: Austin Flint murmur of chronic severe AR
items.explanation: The Austin Flint murmur can be confused with mitral stenosis.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 4)

items.subtopic: Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)
items.question: Which murmur typically becomes louder with inspiration?
items.explanation: Right-sided murmurs and sounds generally increase with inspiration, except for the pulmonic ejection sound (PES).
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)

items.subtopic: Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)
items.question: During which phase of the Valsalva maneuver do most left-sided murmurs decrease in intensity?
items.explanation: Most left-sided heart murmurs decrease in intensity and duration during the strain phase of the Valsalva maneuver.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)

items.subtopic: Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)
items.question: What happens to the murmur of hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy during squatting?
items.explanation: The murmur of HOCM usually softens and may disappear with squatting.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)

items.subtopic: Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)
items.question: Which maneuver is associated with an abrupt increase in left ventricular preload and afterload?
items.explanation: Squatting is associated with an abrupt increase in LV preload and afterload.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)

items.question: Which murmur is indicative of pathologic mitral regurgitation in patients with a mitral bioprosthesis?
options: A grade 1 to 2 midsystolic murmur along the left sternal border
A soft mid-diastolic murmur that occurs with normal LV filling
A high-pitched or holosystolic apical murmur
items.explanation: A high-pitched or holosystolic apical murmur indicates pathologic MR due to a paravalvular leak and/or intra-annular bioprosthetic regurgitation.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Exercise (part 2)

items.question: What is the first clue that may suggest mechanical valve dysfunction?
options: A decrease in the intensity of either the opening or the closing sound
Clinical deterioration with signs of shock
items.explanation: Mechanical valve dysfunction may first be suggested by a decrease in the intensity of either the opening or the closing sound.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Exercise (part 2)

items.question: What is the typical location for hearing a midsystolic murmur associated with a tissue valve in the aortic position?
options: At the base or just below the suprasternal notch
items.explanation: A tissue valve in the aortic position is associated with a grade 1 to 3 midsystolic murmur at the base or just below the suprasternal notch.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Exercise (part 2)

items.subtopic: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System
items.statement: Clinical performance in physical examination improves predictably with experience.
items.explanation: The passage states that clinical performance does not improve predictably with experience.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System

items.subtopic: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System
items.statement: Digital stethoscopes are foundational to AI-assisted evaluation of auscultatory events.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that digital stethoscopes may enhance learning and are foundational for AI-assisted evaluation.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System

items.statement: Peripheral cyanosis is usually related to reduced extremity blood flow due to small vessel constriction.
items.explanation: Peripheral cyanosis or acrocyanosis is typically caused by reduced extremity blood flow from small vessel constriction.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.statement: Arcus senilis pattern is a specific index of coronary heart disease risk.
items.explanation: An arcus senilis pattern lacks specificity as an index of coronary heart disease risk.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.statement: Straight back syndrome is associated with mitral valve prolapse.
items.explanation: Straight back syndrome refers to the loss of the normal kyphosis of the thoracic spine and has been described in patients with mitral valve prolapse.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.statement: Homans' sign is specific for deep venous thrombosis.
items.explanation: A Homans' sign is neither specific nor sensitive for deep venous thrombosis.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.statement: A prominent a wave in the jugular venous waveform indicates reduced right ventricular compliance.
items.explanation: A prominent a wave is seen in patients with reduced right ventricular compliance.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.statement: The jugular venous pressure should rise by at least 3 mmHg with inspiration.
items.explanation: Normally, the venous pressure should fall by at least 3 mmHg with inspiration.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.statement: The abdominogujular reflux maneuver involves applying pressure over the left upper quadrant of the abdomen.
items.explanation: The maneuver requires firm and consistent pressure over the right upper quadrant.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.statement: Blood pressure should be measured with the arm at heart level to ensure accuracy.
items.explanation: Accurate blood pressure measurement requires the arm to be at the level of the heart.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.statement: Orthostatic hypotension can be exacerbated by dehydration.
items.explanation: Dehydration is one of the factors that can exacerbate orthostatic hypotension.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 3)

items.statement: The arterial pulse becomes less rapid and spiking as a function of its distance from the heart.
items.explanation: The arterial pulse usually becomes more rapid and spiking with increasing distance from the heart.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 3)

items.statement: A pulsus paradoxus may be palpable at the brachial artery level when the pressure difference exceeds 15 mmHg.
items.explanation: Pulsus paradoxus can indeed be palpable at the brachial or femoral artery level with a pressure difference exceeding 15 mmHg.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 4)

items.statement: The presence of an arterial bruit is a reliable indicator of the degree of vascular obstruction.
items.explanation: The correlation between the presence of a bruit and the degree of vascular obstruction is poor.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 4)

items.statement: An enlarged LV cavity is indicated by a leftward and upward displacement of the apex beat.
items.explanation: Enlargement of the LV cavity results in a leftward and downward displacement of the apex beat.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 5)

items.statement: A large LV aneurysm may be palpable as an ectopic impulse, often dyskinetic.
items.explanation: A large left ventricular aneurysm can sometimes be felt as a distinct and dyskinetic ectopic impulse.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 5)

items.statement: The intensity of $S_2$ decreases with aortic stenosis and pulmonic stenosis.
items.explanation: The intensity of both $A_{2}$ and $P_{2}$ decreases in aortic stenosis and pulmonic stenosis, respectively.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 1)

items.statement: Reversed or paradoxical splitting of $S_2$ is characterized by the interval narrowing with inspiration.
items.explanation: With reversed or paradoxical splitting, the $A_{2}-P_{2}$ interval narrows with inspiration.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 1)

items.statement: An S4 is present with atrial fibrillation.
items.explanation: An S4 is not present with atrial fibrillation.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 2)

items.statement: A midsystolic murmur due to AS can be difficult to differentiate from aortic sclerosis.
items.explanation: It is sometimes difficult to distinguish aortic sclerosis from more advanced degrees of valve stenosis.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 2)

items.statement: The murmur of mitral regurgitation is best heard over the cardiac apex.
items.explanation: The murmur of MR is indeed best heard over the cardiac apex.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 3)

items.statement: A diastolic murmur indicates a non-structural heart condition.
items.explanation: Diastolic murmurs always signify structural heart disease.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 3)

items.statement: Presystolic accentuation occurs in patients with atrial fibrillation.
items.explanation: Presystolic accentuation is absent in patients with atrial fibrillation.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 4)

items.statement: A continuous murmur typically begins in systole and continues through some portion of diastole.
items.explanation: Continuous murmurs begin in systole, envelop S2, and continue into diastole.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 4)

items.subtopic: Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)
items.statement: The murmur of mitral valve prolapse becomes shorter with the strain phase of the Valsalva maneuver.
items.explanation: The murmur of MVP usually becomes longer and often louder during the strain phase of the Valsalva maneuver.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)

items.subtopic: Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)
items.statement: The murmur of hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy becomes louder with rapid standing.
items.explanation: The murmur of HOCM becomes longer and louder on rapid standing.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)

items.statement: A pericardial friction rub is nearly 100% specific for acute pericarditis.
items.explanation: The specificity of a pericardial friction rub for diagnosing acute pericarditis is nearly 100%.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Exercise (part 2)

items.statement: A diastolic murmur of AR is normal in patients with prosthetic heart valves.
items.explanation: A diastolic murmur of AR is abnormal in any circumstance.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Exercise (part 2)

items.subtopic: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System
items.assertion: Observations regarding heart rate and blood pressure contribute to bedside risk assessment in acute coronary syndromes.
items.reason: These observations can inform clinical decision-making before cardiac biomarker testing results are known.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why heart rate and blood pressure observations are important.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System

items.assertion: Telangiectasias on the lips, tongue, and mucous membranes can be a source of right-to-left shunting.
items.reason: These telangiectasias are part of Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.assertion: A high-arched palate is a feature of Marfan syndrome.
items.reason: It is also characteristic of Loeys-Dietz syndrome.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.assertion: Pectus excavatum can be seen in patients with connective tissue disease syndromes.
items.reason: Thoracic cage abnormalities, including pectus carinatum and pectus excavatum, have been well described among patients with connective tissue disease syndromes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.assertion: Varicosities suggest profound hypoalbuminemia.
items.reason: Lower extremity edema in the absence of jugular venous hypertension may be due to profound hypoalbuminemia as seen in nephrotic syndrome or liver failure.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true. Varicosities suggest venous insufficiency, not hypoalbuminemia.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · THE GENERAL PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.assertion: Kussmaul's sign is defined by a rise or lack of fall in the JVP with inspiration.
items.reason: It is classically associated with constrictive pericarditis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains Kussmaul's sign.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.assertion: The v wave in the jugular venous waveform is accentuated in patients with tricuspid regurgitation.
items.reason: This occurs due to increased right atrial compliance.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; the height of the v wave is determined by right atrial compliance and volume returning to the right atrium.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.assertion: An elevated JVP is a predictor of pulmonary artery wedge pressure >22 mmHg.
items.reason: A right atrial pressure >10 mmHg has an 88% positive predictive value for this condition.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.assertion: The Korotkoff sounds used to define diastolic pressure are always the fifth sound.
items.reason: In patients with chronic severe AR or a large arteriovenous fistula, both phase IV and V sounds should be recorded.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because in certain conditions, both phase IV and V Korotkoff sounds are used.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.assertion: White coat hypertension may lead to sustained hypertension over time.
items.reason: Individuals with white coat hypertension often have elevated clinic blood pressure measurements.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 3)

items.assertion: A bifid pulse can be observed in patients with hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy.
items.reason: A bifid pulse is characterized by two systolic peaks and is also seen in patients on intraaortic balloon counterpulsation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 3)

items.assertion: Pulsus alternans can be associated with an increased risk for arrhythmic events.
items.reason: When pulsus alternans is associated with electrocardiographic T-wave alternans, the risk for an arrhythmic event appears to be increased.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 4)

items.assertion: A visible right upper parasternal pulsation is a normal finding in all patients.
items.reason: It may be suggestive of ascending aortic aneurysm disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true as it correctly identifies the association with ascending aortic aneurysm.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 4)

items.assertion: HOCM may cause a triple cadence beat at the apex.
items.reason: This occurs due to contributions from a palpable $S_{4}$ and two components of the bisferiens systolic pulse.
items.explanation: Both the assertion about HOCM causing a triple cadence beat and the reason involving $S_{4}$ and bisferiens pulse are true, with the latter correctly explaining the former.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIOVASCULAR EXAMINATION (part 5)

items.assertion: The intensity of the first heart sound ($S_1$) is louder in hyperkinetic circulatory states.
items.reason: $S_1$ is classically loud in patients with hyperkinetic circulatory states due to increased LV contractility.
items.explanation: Both $S_1$ being louder in hyperkinetic states and the reason involving LV contractility are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 1)

items.assertion: An ejection sound is always associated with aortic stenosis.
items.reason: Ejection sounds occur due to turbulent flow across semilunar valves during systole.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as ejection sounds are not always associated with aortic stenosis; they can also be present in congenital bicuspid valve disease and other conditions.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 1)

items.assertion: An S3 in older patients signifies heart failure.
items.reason: S3 is a normal finding in children, adolescents, and young adults but indicates pathology in older individuals.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 2)

items.assertion: A midsystolic murmur is always indicative of severe AS.
items.reason: AS is a common cause of a midsystolic murmur in adults.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not correctly explain that a midsystolic murmur is always indicative of severe AS.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 2)

items.assertion: The murmur of a ventricular septal defect is holosystolic and loudest at the mid-left sternal border.
items.reason: A thrill is usually present at this location in VSD without significant pulmonary hypertension.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 3)

items.assertion: The murmur of tricuspid regurgitation increases in intensity with expiration.
items.reason: Carvallo's sign indicates that TR murmurs increase in intensity with inspiration.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the murmur of TR actually increases in intensity with inspiration.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC AUSCULTATION (part 3)

items.subtopic: Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)
items.assertion: The murmur of aortic stenosis can be suggested by an increase in intensity after a premature beat.
items.reason: This is due to enhanced left ventricular filling and postextrasystolic potentiation of contractile function.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Physical Examination of the Cardiovascular System · Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)

items.subtopic: Respiration | Valsalva Maneuver | After VPB or AF | Positional Changes | Exercise (part 1)
items.assertion: The murmur of mitral regurgitation increases in intensity after a premature beat.
items.reason: There is a significant change in the left ventricular to left atrial pressure gradient.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the intensity of the murmur of MR does not change after a premature beat.
